stimulus: Phage-display peptides—short protein fragments engineered on the surface of bacteriophages to recognize and stick to specific molecular targets—can be used to detect harmful microbes in beverages, though how reliably they avoid false alarms (their specificity, i.e., the probability of giving a negative result when the target microbe is absent) in real-world drinks has remained unclear. Diana Marquez and colleagues created a strip that incorporates phage-display peptides designed to recognize strain ST131 of the bacterium Klebsiella pneumoniae; the strip turns from yellow to green when the peptides bind to their target. The team tested the strip in store-bought apple cider that had been spiked with K. pneumoniae ST131, with other strains of K. pneumoniae, or with other bacterial species. After exposure, the strip from the ST131 sample turned green while the strips from the other samples remained yellow, suggesting that ______blank
stem: Which choice most logically completes the text?

answerOptions.content: phage-display tests in real-world drinks are more likely to show a high degree of specificity when the target microbe is K. pneumoniae ST131 than when the target is another strain of K. pneumoniae or another species.
the phage-display peptides successfully bound to K. pneumoniae ST131 and the strip displayed a high degree of specificity in a real-world drink.
uncertainty about the specificity of phage-display tests for microbes in real-world drinks may stem from similarities between K. pneumoniae ST131 and other K. pneumoniae strains.
the specificity of the strip in a real-world drink was unaffected by the peptides’ tendency to bind to multiple strains of K. pneumoniae.

rationale:
Choice B is the best answer because it most logically completes the text's discussion of phage-display peptides used to detect microbes in beverages. The strip turned green when exposed to the targeted strain (ST131) but stayed yellow when exposed to other strains of K. pneumoniae or other bacterial species. This pattern indicates two things: the peptides did bind to their intended target, and the strip yielded a correct negative result whenever the target was absent—in other words, a high degree of specificity in a real-world drink.

Choice A is incorrect because nothing in the text indicates that ST131 differs from other targets in a way that would make phage-display tests more specific for ST131 than for other microbes. Choice C is incorrect because similarities between ST131 and other K. pneumoniae strains aren't discussed, and the results actually show the peptides distinguishing ST131 from other strains. Choice D is incorrect because the results suggest the peptides did not bind to multiple strains—they bound only to ST131. If they had bound to other strains too, specificity would have been compromised, not preserved.
